frameworks.name: Bayesian observer
frameworks.one_line: Full Bayesian inference serves as the optimal accuracy benchmark.

frameworks.name: self-consistent conditioning (confirmation bias)
frameworks.one_line: Estimation conditioned on a self-generated categorical commitment about the stimulus.

frameworks.name: hierarchical perceptual inference
frameworks.one_line: Two-stage categorical-then-feature inference under overlapping category distributions.

core_question: Why do observers condition feature estimates on their own categorical interpretation when doing so double-counts sensory evidence?
key_assumptions: Stimulus feature belongs to one of two categories with overlapping distributions
Observer first commits to a category, then estimates the feature
Prior expectations reflect statistical structure of the world
manipulations: Inference strategy (self-consistent conditioning vs full Bayesian)
Feature value across the stimulus range
Additional sensory noise injected after categorical commitment
key_findings: Self-consistent conditioning is globally suboptimal compared to full Bayesian observer
Accuracy loss is non-uniform across the feature range
Conditioning yields higher accuracy where categorical-assignment uncertainty is low
Benefits of conditioning grow when sensory information degrades after the categorical commitment
interpretations: Confirmation-bias-like conditioning can be locally adaptive despite global suboptimality
Self-generated priors trade global accuracy for robustness under post-decision noise
